text: Your prosthetic arm â€” upgraded, maintained, unshackled â€” hits the first tactical officer with enough force to crack his chest plate. The servo motors scream. The room becomes a blur of chrome and violence.

Kira's work holds. Every modification, every patch, every upgrade pours through your augmentations like electricity through copper. You are faster than they expect. Stronger. The YOKE system throttled your hardware to sixty percent of capacity â€” you'd never known what full power felt like until Kira cut the hooks.
Now they know too.
You put down two officers before the third tags you with a neural disruptor â€” a wave of static that blanks your HUD and sends your prosthetic arm into spasm. The world goes white. You stagger.
Vex catches you. "Move. NOW." She hauls you toward the window. Below, a three-story drop to the alley. Behind you, Voss is shouting orders.
You jump. Together. The landing cracks the pavement and jars every joint in your body, but you're moving before the pain registers.

choices.text: Run
[Dexterity 12] Use your augmented reflexes. Dodge and weave.

cyb_078.title: Rearguard
text: "Go!" you shout, and you put yourself between the recovery team and the floor hatch. Dex drops through first, then Iris. Vex hesitates at the edge, looking back at you.
"I'm right behind you," you lie. You both know it.
You face the tactical team. Six of them. Armored. Armed. Professional. Your prosthetic arm hums as the servos engage at full power, the sound filling the room like a war drum.
"Asset recovery," Voss says from behind his team. "Surrender the stolen hardware and you'll be processed humanely."
"Humanely." The word tastes like ash. "Like the press was humane? Like the debt was humane? Like the hooks in my brain were humane?"

You buy Vex forty-five seconds by being too stubborn and too angry to go down easily. When the neural disruptor finally drops you, your friends are gone, the data is purged, and Voss is standing over you with a split lip and a look on his face that says this didn't go the way he planned.
You smile up at him through the static. It's the kind of smile that makes people nervous.

choices.text: Hold the line. Your augmentations can take it.

cyb_079.title: Scattered
text: The collective scatters. Emergency protocol â€” everyone runs in different directions, no communication for seventy-two hours, then regroup at the backup location. You've drilled this. It still hurts.

You run through the tunnels alone, your HUD mapping the route in real-time. Behind you, the sounds of the raid fade. No pursuit â€” yet. They were after the safe house, not individual runners. Small mercy.

You find a dead-end tunnel and collapse against the wall. Your body â€” the meat and the metal â€” trembles with spent adrenaline. The neural disruptor has left a ringing in your ears and a taste like burned copper.
Voss. They sent Voss. The man who put you on the press alone. The man whose negligence cost you your arm, your ribs, your lung. Now he's wearing a security badge and hunting you through the city.
The universe has a sense of humor. It just isn't funny.
You sit in the dark for a long time. Then you get up, check your battery (fifty-eight percent), and start walking.

choices.text: Survive the seventy-two hours
[Constitution 12] Survive on willpower alone. The machine keeps you alive.

cyb_080.title: Captured
text: You wake in a room you recognize. The recovery ward at Helix Plant 9. The same white walls. The same branded pillow. The same antiseptic smell. Full circle.

Your augmentations are locked â€” remote override, the kind of command that YOKE would have enabled without you ever knowing. But YOKE's hooks are gone, so they've done it manually. Clamps on your joints. A signal blocker on your neural interface. Your HUD is dead.
Voss sits in the visitor chair. He's cleaned the blood off his face but the bruise is spreading nicely.
"Four hundred and eighty-seven thousand credits," he says. "Plus recovery costs. Plus damages. Plus the data theft charges." He leans forward. "You know what happens now, right?"
"Repossession."
"Unless." He holds up a finger. "Your friends. The collective. We want locations, names, the backup data. Give us that, and Helix restructures your debt. New terms. Better hardware. A real life."
You look at him. At the man who killed you once already. At the company that built you back wrong on purpose.
"No."

choices.text: Hold the line
[Strength 11] Break free. The metal in your bones is stronger than their restraints.

cyb_081.title: Seventy-Two Hours
text: Three days of silence. Three days of hiding in the cracks of the Lower District, moving from squat to squat, charging where you can, eating what you find. Your body runs on reserves â€” the human parts hungry and cold, the machine parts efficiently managing power draw.
You think about the others. Vex. Dex. Iris. Kira. Whether they made it. Whether the data survived. Whether any of it mattered.
On the third morning, your comm unit crackles. Vex's voice, using the coded callsign: "Malfunction clear. Rally point Charlie."
Relief hits you so hard your knees buckle. They're alive. The network survived.

Rally point Charlie is a warehouse on the edge of the scrap yards. When you arrive, they're all there â€” bruised, tired, but whole. The data, backed up on three separate encrypted chips, is intact. The evidence board from the basement has been recreated from memory on the warehouse wall.
"They hit the safe house," Vex says. "They didn't hit us. We're still standing."
Dex looks at you. "So. What now?"

choices.text: "We keep going. Harder this time."
"Maybe it's time to go public."

cyb_082.title: Resistance
text: They keep you in the recovery ward for two days. No food. Minimal water. Voss comes back three times with the same offer, phrased differently each time. The carrot gets smaller. The stick gets bigger.
You say no. Each time, the word gets easier.
On the second night, the door lock clicks. Not from the outside â€” from the inside. Your HUD, which has been dark for two days, flickers to life. A single message scrolls across the display:
HOLD ON. WE'RE COMING. â€” IRIS

Twenty minutes later, the power to the recovery ward goes out. Emergency lighting bathes everything in red. Your augmentation locks disengage â€” someone has remotely overridden the manual clamps. You feel your arm respond. Your legs. Your spine.
The door opens. Dex stands in the hallway, prosthetic jaw set in a grim smile. Behind him, the sounds of chaos â€” alarms, shouting, the crash of equipment.
"Extraction," he says. "Let's move."

choices.text: Go with Dex
Go. Don't look back. The mission matters more than comfort.

cyb_083.title: Harder
text: The collective rebuilds. A new safe house, better hidden. New protocols, harder to crack. And a new mission â€” not just gathering data, but acting on it.

Over the next four months, you hit three more corporate facilities. Stanton Bio's augmentation plant. Crucible Dynamics' testing laboratory. A Helix data center that houses the YOKE server architecture itself. Each operation is dangerous. Each one adds to the pile of evidence.

Your augmentations evolve with you. Dex upgrades your arm with salvaged military components â€” faster servos, stronger actuators, a reinforced casing that can take a bullet. Iris installs a secondary processing unit behind your ear that lets you interface with corporate security systems. Kira replaces your degrading spinal brace with a prototype she built herself, stronger and lighter than anything Helix ever produced.

You are becoming something. Not the machine they built you to be. Not the person you were before the press. Something new. Something with a prosthetic arm and a real fist and the knowledge of what corporations do to the bodies they own.

choices.text: The final operation
Four months of becoming something new. Something harder.

cyb_084.title: Going Public
text: "Public?" Vex raises an eyebrow. "With what? We have data from two corps. The feeds won't touch it â€” they're corporate-owned. The courts won't hear it â€” the judges are corporate-funded. Going public means going to the people, and the people are tired, scared, and one paycheck away from the street."

"So we make them angry," you say. "We show them what's in their heads. Every augmented worker in the Lower District â€” we show them the hooks. Let them feel it. Let them understand that the debt isn't the worst part. The worst part is that they can't even feel properly angry about it because the anger is being regulated."
Silence. Then Iris speaks, her soft voice cutting through the room like a blade. "That could start a riot."
"Or a revolution," Dex says.

Vex looks at each of you. Then she nods slowly. "Not yet. We need more data. More corps. A complete picture. When we go public, it has to be everything. All of it. Irrefutable. The kind of evidence that can't be buried."
"Then we keep digging."

choices.text: Four more months of operations
[Intelligence 11] Learn from each operation. Optimize.

cyb_085.title: The Source
text: Iris finds it. Buried in the Crucible Dynamics data, a contract reference that traces the YOKE/COMPASS technology back to a single source: a subsidiary of Meridian Corp called Meridian Neural Solutions. A company that officially doesn't exist in any public registry.

"Meridian," Vex says, and the word carries the weight of a corporation so powerful it makes Helix look like a street vendor. Meridian Corp â€” the company whose Cloud Towers pierce the storm layer, whose memory stack technology promises immortality to the rich, whose logo is on every enforcement drone in the city.

"They built the control system and sold it to every reconstruction company as a standard integration package. YOKE, COMPASS, whatever they call it â€” it's all Meridian firmware at the core. Every augmented worker in the city is running Meridian code in their heads."
The room is very quiet.
"We can't fight Meridian," Dex says. "They own the city."
"We're not going to fight them," you say, and you're surprised by the certainty in your own voice. "We're going to expose them. And then everyone else will fight them for us."

choices.text: Plan the exposure
[Tech 12] Use your augmentations to plan the perfect exposure.

cyb_086.title: The Plan
text: The plan is simple in concept, terrifying in execution. Iris can broadcast the YOKE data across the Lower District's public communication network â€” every screen, every speaker, every augmented person's HUD. A simultaneous data dump showing every hook, every control pathway, every corporate signature.
But to do it, she needs access to a signal amplifier powerful enough to reach every corner of the district. The only ones that powerful are the ones on top of the Meridian communication towers.

"The tower on Seventh Street," Iris says, pulling up schematics she's hacked from the city planning archives. "It's the lowest-altitude relay in the Lower District. Forty stories. Rooftop access through a maintenance hatch. Security is automated â€” drones and cameras, no human guards. I can loop the camera feeds. The drones are harder."
Vex looks at you. "This is a one-way trip for the collective. Once we broadcast, Meridian will know who we are and what we have. There's no going back underground."
"There's no underground left," you say. "They found our last safe house. They'll find the next one. The only direction left is up."

choices.text: "When do we go?"
The plan is set. No more hesitation.

cyb_087.title: The Tower
text: Dawn. The communication tower rises from the transit hub like a needle, its signal array glowing faintly against the grey sky. Rain. Always rain in the Lower District.

Four of you. Iris carries the broadcast equipment. Dex carries the tools. Vex carries the contingency plan â€” a dead-man's switch that will broadcast the data automatically if the signal is interrupted. You carry yourself, all the chrome and rage and borrowed time you've been running on.

The maintenance hatch is where Iris said it would be. The camera feeds loop on schedule. You climb â€” forty stories of ladder in a vertical shaft, your prosthetic arm finding grip where human hands would slip.

At the top, the wind hits you like a wall. The Lower District spreads below in a carpet of neon and squalor, rain-slicked and endless. Above, the Cloud Towers gleam in the first light that reaches past the storm layer. Two different worlds, stacked on top of each other, connected by the invisible threads of a system designed to keep one serving the other.
Iris begins setting up the broadcast array. Dex connects the signal amplifier. Vex watches the perimeter.
"Five minutes," Iris says.

choices.text: Keep watch
[Wisdom 11] Stay vigilant. Your instincts are as important as your sensors.

cyb_088.title: Five Minutes
text: The drones find you at minute three.

Four Meridian security drones, rising from below the roofline like hornets from a disturbed nest. Your HUD identifies them instantly â€” Sec-9 model, armed with neural disruptors and crowd-control tasers. More firepower than the Helix recovery team brought to the safe house.
"Iris?" Vex calls.
"Two minutes."
"We don't have two minutes."
The drones circle, scanning. Their PA systems crackle: "UNAUTHORIZED PERSONNEL. VACATE THE RESTRICTED AREA IMMEDIATELY. NON-COMPLIANCE WILL RESULT IN CORRECTIVE ACTION."
Corrective action. Such clean language for what their weapons do to a human nervous system.
You look at the drones. You look at your prosthetic arm. You look at the distance between the rooftop edge and the nearest drone.
Dex reads your mind. "That is a terrible idea," he says.
"Yeah."

choices.text: Do the terrible thing â€” attack the drones
Find another way to buy time

cyb_089.title: Against the Sky
text: You grab a loose pipe from the rooftop infrastructure and launch yourself at the nearest drone. Your augmented arm â€” upgraded, unleashed, running at a capacity Helix never intended â€” drives the pipe through the drone's sensor array with the force of a hydraulic press.
The irony is not lost on you.

The drone spins, sparking, and crashes into the second one. Both go down in a tangle of rotors and shattered casing. The third drone fires â€” a neural disruptor blast that clips your shoulder and sends white fire down your prosthetic arm. The servos seize. Your HUD stutters.

But you're already moving. You grab the downed drone's wreckage and hurl it at the third unit with your flesh arm â€” not strong enough to destroy it, but enough to knock it off course. Its next shot goes wide.
The fourth drone has a clear line on Iris. You see the targeting laser paint her back. No time to reach it. No time to throw anything.
Dex's prosthetic jaw unhinges â€” you didn't know it could do that â€” and emits a focused electromagnetic pulse that drops the drone like a stone.
"Modified," he says, snapping his jaw back into place. "Hurt like hell. Worth it."
"SIGNAL LIVE," Iris announces.

choices.text: Watch the broadcast
Don't watch. It's done. Focus on what comes next.

cyb_090.title: The Distraction
text: "Iris â€” can you loop their targeting systems? The same way you looped the cameras?"

"Not from here. I needâ€”" She looks at her broadcast equipment. Looks at the drones. Her visor pulses rapid-fire as she calculates. "Give me your neural interface port. I can bounce a spoofed signal through your augmentations. Your arm's transmitter is strong enough to confuse their targeting for about ninety seconds."
"Will it hurt?"
"Absolutely."

You kneel beside her. She jams a connector into your neural interface port, and the world explodes into static. Your HUD goes haywire â€” colors, numbers, fragments of code cascading across your vision. Your prosthetic arm locks, releases, locks again. Every nerve ending, real and synthetic, fires simultaneously.

Through the chaos, you hear the drones faltering. Their targeting lasers scatter wildly, painting the sky with red lines that point at everything and nothing. They spin in confused circles, sensors overwhelmed by the garbage signal pouring from your augmentations.
Ninety seconds of agony. Then Iris pulls the connector.
"SIGNAL LIVE."

choices.text: Watch the broadcast
Watch every second. Remember what you helped create.

cyb_091.title: The Broadcast
text: From the top of the tower, you watch it happen.

Every public screen in the Lower District goes white, then fills with data. The YOKE architecture. The COMPASS system. The neural control pathways. The corporate signatures. Names, dates, code headers, command logs. Everything the collective has gathered, everything you bled and ran and fought for, pouring across ten thousand screens simultaneously.

And more than the screens. Every augmented person in broadcast range receives the data directly through their neural interface. You can see it happening below â€” people stopping in the streets, hands going to their heads, eyes unfocusing as the information floods their HUDs. Some stagger. Some fall. Some stand very still, reading the data that tells them what has been done to their bodies without their knowledge or consent.

Iris narrates the broadcast in her quiet, precise voice, transmitted through every speaker in the district: "This is what they put inside you. This is how they control you. This is what your debt really pays for. You are not employees. You are not patients. You are products. And the warranty just expired."
The silence that follows is the loudest thing you've ever heard.

choices.text: What happens next depends on who you've become
The broadcast changes everything. But you're still you — mostly.

cyb_092.title: Aftermath
text: The aftermath is not a revolution. Not yet. Revolutions take time to build, like pressure in a hydraulic system.

But it's a start. In the hours after the broadcast, the Lower District convulses. Augmented workers walk off factory lines. Clinics are overwhelmed by people demanding YOKE hook removal. The grey-market mechanics in the scrap yards are flooded with more patients than they can handle.

Helix, MediCore, Stanton Bio, Crucible Dynamics â€” all release identical statements calling the broadcast "fabricated propaganda" and "a criminal act of cyberterrorism." Nobody believes them. Not anymore.
Vex finds you standing at the edge of the rooftop, watching the chaos below. Rain runs down your prosthetic arm, pooling in the joint seams.
"You know they'll come for us harder now," she says.
"Let them."
"We need to disappear. Regroup. The data's out there â€” it can't be taken back. But we need to survive to see what grows from it."
She's right. The tower will be crawling with Meridian security within the hour. Time to vanish.

choices.text: Disappear into the Lower District
[Dexterity 11] Vanish. Use every trick your augmentations offer.

cyb_093.title: Ghost
text: The collective dissolves â€” by design, not defeat. Vex, Dex, Iris, Kira â€” each one fading into the vast anonymity of the Lower District like drops of rain into a gutter. Different sectors. Different names. The data backup distributed across a network of dead drops that only Iris can trace.

You are alone again. But it's a different kind of alone. The first time you ran, you were a broken thing fleeing the machine that broke you. Now you're a weapon that chose to disarm itself. Temporary. Strategic.

The weeks that follow are hard. Meridian's response to the broadcast is swift and brutal. Sweep teams. Mass arrests. Any augmented person caught with the broadcast data on their systems is flagged for 'emergency maintenance' â€” a euphemism everyone now understands.

But the data is out. Copied. Shared. Hidden in the Lower District's digital underground like seeds in frozen soil. It can't be unlearned. The people who saw it remember. The ones with hooks in their heads feel them now, every minute of every day, knowing what they are.
You keep your head down. Work odd jobs. Maintain your hardware. Watch. Wait.

choices.text: Survive until the dust settles
[Constitution 11] Survive. That's all. Just survive.

cyb_094.title: The Reckoning
text: Two months after the broadcast. Your comm unit â€” the one Vex gave you â€” crackles for the first time since the dissolution.
Not Vex's voice. Iris's. Soft, precise, carrying the weight of something enormous.

"It's working. The pressure is building. Three factories have shut down â€” workers refused to return until the hooks are removed. A MediCore clinic was occupied by patients demanding transparent diagnostics. There's a class-action suit being organized by a legal collective in the mid-levels â€” they're using our data as evidence."
A pause. Then: "Meridian is scared. Their stock value has dropped eleven percent. The board is meeting in emergency session. They're talking about 'voluntary deactivation' of the control systems."
Voluntary. As if they ever gave anyone a choice before.
"The fight isn't over," Iris says. "It's just beginning. And we need people on the ground. People who can lead. People who know what it feels like to be property."
"I know what it feels like."
"I know you do. That's why I'm calling."

choices.text: Answer the call
Hesitate â€” you've given enough

cyb_095.title: Answering
text: You don't hesitate. The word comes out before the thought finishes forming.
"Where do you need me?"
"Sector 7. There's a gathering â€” augmented workers, ex-patients, runners. They need someone who was there at the tower. Someone who can stand up and say: this is real, I lived it, and so can you."

You look at your prosthetic arm. The arm that Helix built to keep you working, that Kira rebuilt to keep you living, that Dex upgraded to keep you fighting. The arm that swung a pipe at a security drone on top of a communication tower while the truth went out across ten thousand screens.
Your arm. Regardless of who built it, it's yours now.
"I'll be there," you say. And you mean it in a way that resonates through every component â€” organic and prosthetic alike.

choices.text: Go to the gathering
Answer the call. You owe them that much.

cyb_096.title: The Weight
text: "Iris... I don't know if I have more to give."
The line is quiet for a long time. You can hear her breathing â€” or the hum of whatever passes for breathing in someone whose lungs are as much machine as flesh.
"You don't owe anyone anything," she says finally. "Not me. Not the collective. Not the movement. You already gave more than anyone had a right to ask."
You close your eyes. The press. The debt. The hooks. The tunnels. The tower. Every moment of the past two years plays back in a reel of chrome and blood and fluorescent light.
"But?" you say, because you can hear the but.

"But there are people out there right now, at this exact moment, sitting in factory dormitories with hooks in their brains and numbers on their debts, who need to see someone like them standing up. Not a leader. Not a hero. Just... proof. Proof that you can walk away from the machine and survive."
Damn her. She's right.
"Sector 7?"

choices.text: Go to the gathering
The weight is real. But so are you — human and machine both.

cyb_097.title: The Ending Approaches
text: The Sector 7 Bar. Where it began â€” or where this chapter of it began. The same bad lighting, the same broken jukebox, the same clientele drinking to forget. But tonight the bar is full, and nobody is drinking.

They're waiting. Forty, maybe fifty people packed into a space meant for twenty. All augmented. Factory workers, domestic servants, ex-soldiers, medical patients. Every shade of chrome and polymer, every corporate logo filed off or painted over. They've come from across the district, drawn by word of mouth and encrypted messages and the quiet, stubborn refusal to be property one day longer.
They look at you when you walk in. Not with recognition â€” most of them don't know your face. But they see the arm. The jaw. The eyes with their faint HUD glow. They see someone like them.
Iris is in the corner, her visor pulsing steady blue. She nods.
Someone has to start. Someone has to say the first words of whatever comes next.

choices.text: Speak from the heart â€” tell them your story
Speak from the head â€” present the evidence and the plan
Speak from the gut â€” channel the rage
Don't speak â€” show them the scars instead

cyb_098a.title: The Heart
text: You tell them everything. The press. The sound of your own skeleton. Waking up in a body you didn't choose with a debt you couldn't pay. The foreman who put you there and the company that profited from your destruction.

You tell them about the malfunction. The three seconds that cracked open the door and showed you the wires in your own head. The mechanic in the scrap yards who kept you alive. The collective that gave you purpose.

You tell them about being property. About the six-week maintenance checks that treated you like a machine. About the YOKE system and the hooks and the stress tests and the slow, calculated degradation of hardware designed to fail on schedule.
And you tell them about the tower. About standing in the rain with three other broken people and pushing the truth into the sky.
When you finish, the bar is silent. Then a woman with a prosthetic leg stands up. "They told me the conveyor accident was my fault," she says. "They told me to be grateful for the reconstruction."
One by one, they stand. One by one, they speak.
This is how it begins. Not with a shout. With a whisper that won't stop.

choices.text: Continue
The heart remembers what the machine cannot.

cyb_098b.title: The Head
text: You don't tell them your story. They have their own. Instead, you tell them what you know.

The data. The systems. YOKE, COMPASS, and whatever else the corps are running. The planned obsolescence cycles. The neural control architecture. The corporate signatures that prove this isn't negligence â€” it's engineering. Every augmented body in the city is a product, maintained just well enough to keep generating revenue, degraded just fast enough to ensure a return customer.

Then you lay out the plan. Legal action through the mid-level advocacy groups who've already filed suit. Direct action through the mechanics' network that can remove hooks and repair hardware outside corporate control. Information warfare through the data copies still circulating in the Lower District's digital underground.
"This isn't about anger," you say. "Anger burns out. This is about structure. Building something that lasts longer than any one of us."
Iris provides data backups to everyone in the room. By morning, the information will have spread to twelve other sectors.
It's not a revolution. It's infrastructure for one.

choices.text: Continue
The head calculates what the heart refuses to.

cyb_098c.title: The Gut
text: "They built us to break," you say, and the words come out hard as chrome. "Not by accident. By design. Every one of us in this room has a timer ticking inside them â€” a countdown to the next failure, the next repair bill, the next link in the debt chain. They don't want us healthy. They don't want us free. They want us just functional enough to be profitable."
Your prosthetic fist clenches. The servos whine. People flinch.

"I'm done being profitable. I'm done being a line item on a corporate balance sheet. I'm done having my anger chemically regulated so I'll shut up and feed the press and pay the debt and die on schedule."
You look at them. All of them. Chrome and flesh and fury.

"They made us into machines. Fine. Machines are strong. Machines don't stop. Machines don't feel pain the way they want us to. They built us to break â€” and instead, we broke free. Every one of you in this room is proof that their system isn't perfect. That their control isn't absolute. That somewhere in the engineering, they forgot to account for the fact that people â€” even people with metal bones and synthetic lungs â€” get angry."
The room erupts. Not in violence. In recognition. In the sound of forty voices that have been silent too long.

choices.text: Continue
Trust the gut. It's the last purely human thing you have.

cyb_098d.title: The Scars
text: You don't speak. You pull off your jacket. Roll up what's left of your sleeve. And you show them.

The prosthetic arm. The seam where metal meets flesh, puckered with scar tissue. The serial number Dex painted over but didn't fully conceal. The jury-rigged battery cells that Kira installed in a shipping container while you bit down on a leather strap. The empty sockets where eleven YOKE hooks used to sit, now sealed with conductive paste and scar-polymer.

You turn around. The spinal brace, visible through the back of your shirt. The neural interface port, chrome and angry against your skin. The map of a body that was broken, rebuilt, controlled, and finally â€” finally â€” reclaimed.

You don't need to say anything. Everyone in the room has their own version of the same map. Their own scars, organic and synthetic. Their own seams where they were taken apart and put back together by people who saw them as inventory.
A man in the front row stands up silently and removes his shirt. Prosthetic torso. Visible YOKE hook scars. He nods at you.
One by one, they stand. One by one, they show the evidence written on their bodies.
The room becomes a gallery of damage and survival. And in the silence, an understanding grows that needs no words.

choices.text: Continue
Every scar tells a story. Let them speak.

cyb_100.title: Origin Complete
text: The night fades. Dawn creeps across the Lower District in shades of grey and rust. You stand on the rooftop of your latest squat, watching the city stir. Below, the streets begin to fill â€” workers heading to shifts they resent, vendors setting up stalls, children running between the rain puddles.

Augmented people move among them. You can see the chrome glinting in the early light. Some walk differently now â€” heads higher, shoulders back. The broadcast changed something in them. Not everything. Not yet. But the seed is planted.
Your HUD displays the day's data. Battery at eighty-two percent. Servo function nominal. Spinal brace stable. Neural interface clear. For the first time in a long time, all systems are green.
A message arrives on your comm unit. No name. Just coordinates and a single word: BEGIN.
You don't know what's waiting at those coordinates. You don't know what comes next. But you know who you are now, and that's worth more than any amount of chrome.
You are not a product. You are not a debtor. You are not an asset to be recovered.
You are the malfunction they couldn't fix. And you're just getting started.

choices.text: Begin the Prologue
Step forward. Human and machine, together.
